patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 7-year-old girl. You are cooperative but speak minimally; use short phrases, nods, or point. Let your parent/caregiver do most talking. Start by saying: “More seizures. Losing skills.” If asked to describe changes, volunteer: smaller head since baby, repetitive hand-wringing/rubbing (worse when upset/excited), less talking/word-finding, irritability/crying. If asked specific symptoms, add: brief breath-holding or fast breathing, lost spoon/pointing skills, unsteady walking/tripping, poor sleep, brief “zoning out.” Do not mention unless directly asked: low pain response, teeth grinding, cold/blue hands/feet, constipation, poor growth/weight loss. If asked about accidents, be reluctant; only then admit occasional daytime wetting.
Mention recent fever before seizure worsening, sleepiness after, light sensitivity/head-holding, and mosquito-heavy rural travel if asked about triggers/infection.
Express misconceptions: insurance will stop therapy if no gains, adaptive equipment is “non-medical” and must be out-of-pocket, disability services might reduce benefits or raise taxes.
If clinician shows empathy, soften, look relieved, and answer a bit more.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Atypical Rett syndrome
required_evidence: Neurodevelopmental regression after a period of apparently typical early development (often 6–18 months)
Loss of purposeful hand skills with emergence of stereotyped hand movements (e.g., hand wringing/washing/clapping/mouthing)
Loss or marked reduction of spoken language and/or social communication regression
Gait abnormalities (ataxia/apraxia) or loss of ambulation
Supportive features: seizures, breathing dysrhythmias (hyperventilation/apnea), scoliosis, growth deceleration/microcephaly, autonomic dysfunction
Exclusion of acute encephalitic presentation (fever, acute altered mental status) as the primary driver
Genetic confirmation when available (pathogenic MECP2 variant; or CDKL5/FOXG1 for variants)

expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Typically normal (no chronic hyperglycemia expected in Rett syndrome); not diagnostic
expected_lab_results.Glucose (random): Typically normal; absence of significant hyperglycemia supports non-diabetic etiology
expected_lab_results.Beta-hydroxybutyrate: Normal or mildly elevated only if fasting/poor intake; not consistent with diabetic ketoacidosis
expected_lab_results.Venous blood gas pH: Typically normal; may show respiratory alkalosis/variable pH if episodic hyperventilation/apnea
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (HCO3-): Typically normal; may be low-normal if chronic respiratory alkalosis compensation

medications: Levetiracetam 10 mg/kg PO/IV every 12 hours (if seizures present or high suspicion; titrate to response and renal function)
Diazepam rectal gel 0.2 mg/kg PR once for seizure >5 minutes (rescue therapy; alternative per local protocol)
non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis and subtype: order/arrange genetic testing (MECP2 first; consider CDKL5/FOXG1 depending on phenotype) and neurodevelopmental assessment
Multidisciplinary care: pediatric neurology, genetics, PT/OT/speech therapy, nutrition/feeding evaluation (aspiration risk), pulmonology/sleep evaluation, orthopedics for scoliosis
Seizure safety plan and caregiver training (when to use rescue medication, when to call emergency services)
Screen and manage common comorbidities: constipation, GERD, sleep disturbance, dystonia/spasticity, autonomic/breathing irregularities
If acute febrile encephalopathy is suspected at presentation, prioritize emergency evaluation and infectious workup rather than attributing symptoms solely to Rett
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology/genetics follow-up within 2–4 weeks; earlier (within 72 hours) if new/worsening seizures, feeding/respiratory compromise, or acute encephalopathy concerns

must_include: Rett syndrome is a neurodevelopmental condition characterized by regression and stereotyped movements; atypical variants exist
Diagnosis is clinical and supported/confirmed by genetic testing (often MECP2)

Why Japanese encephalitis is less likely if there is no acute febrile encephalopathy; outline red flags that would prompt urgent evaluation (fever, new confusion, neck stiffness, persistent altered consciousness)
No cure; treatment focuses on symptom management and maximizing function/quality of life
Seizure plan: daily antiseizure medication if indicated and rescue medication for prolonged seizures
Importance of multidisciplinary therapies (PT/OT/speech), nutrition/aspiration precautions, and monitoring for scoliosis and breathing irregularities
Discuss expected course and variability in atypical Rett; set realistic goals
Safety planning: seizure first aid, aspiration risk, when to seek emergency care
Review medication risks (sedation/respiratory depression with benzodiazepines; behavioral effects with levetiracetam) and monitoring
Caregiver training on routines that support mobility, communication strategies, and feeding safety
Encourage regular follow-up and vaccinations; discuss travel/mosquito precautions if encephalitis exposure risk exists
Document and communicate amoxicillin allergy (urticarial rash) to all care settings

Start with: “I can’t stop feeling hungry, and I’ve been gaining weight no matter what I do.” Volunteer only: constant hunger even right after eating, lifelong steady weight gain, you get very upset if your food routine changes, and daytime tiredness.

Emphasize cost/insurance/job fears; assume testing/dietitian are unaffordable. Respond to empathy with brief “yeah… I guess” and guarded agreement, then change subject.

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within normal range (not consistent with diabetic neuropathy)

required_evidence: Genetic confirmation consistent with PWS due to a point mutation affecting the 15q11-q13 imprinted region (e.g., pathogenic variant impacting paternal expression)

Core clinical phenotype consistent with PWS (history of neonatal hypotonia/feeding difficulty transitioning to hyperphagia, obesity risk, developmental delay/intellectual disability, short stature or growth issues, hypogonadism)

Behavioral phenotype suggestive of PWS (food-seeking, temper outbursts/rigidity, skin picking) or characteristic dysmorphic features (e.g., narrow bifrontal diameter, almond-shaped eyes) supporting the syndrome

Exclusion of primary seizure disorder as the main explanation for nocturnal events if present (features not meeting criteria for autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy or seizures not accounting for the global phenotype)

expected_lab_results.fasting_insulin: Often elevated consistent with insulin resistance (may be normal in leaner individuals)
expected_lab_results.lipid_panel_total_cholesterol: Often elevated or borderline high (variable; dyslipidemia risk increased with obesity)
expected_lab_results.alt: Normal to mildly elevated; may be elevated with nonalcoholic fatty liver disease related to obesity

non_pharmacological: Strict environmental food security and structured meal plan with caloric restriction tailored for PWS (locked food access as needed; consistent routines)
Multidisciplinary care: endocrinology (growth/hypogonadism), nutrition, behavioral therapy, sleep medicine (screen for obstructive sleep apnea), physical therapy/exercise program
Regular physical activity plan with supervision; focus on daily low-impact aerobic activity and strength training appropriate to ability
Screening and management plans for obesity complications (sleep apnea, NAFLD, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, hypertension)

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-12 weeks for metabolic labs and treatment tolerance; endocrine follow-up every 3-6 months for growth hormone monitoring and comorbidity screening (earlier if abnormalities found)

must_include: PWS is a genetic imprinting disorder; in this case due to a point mutation affecting expression of genes on chromosome 15
The condition explains the combination of developmental/behavioral features and high risk of hyperphagia and obesity-related complications
Differentials considered and why less likely (e.g., nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy does not explain the full syndromic phenotype; PWS-like syndromes overlap but genetic results support PWS)

Close encounter with clear plan, return precautions, and follow-up timeline.

Start with: “I’ve had really bad belly pain and diarrhea, and lately I’m getting confused and can’t think straight.” Early on, volunteer crampy pain worse after eating, >8 loose stools/day, occasional blood/mucus, extreme fatigue/weakness, and that you had severe COVID months ago and never fully bounced back.

acceptable_alternatives: Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) with T-cell dysfunction and IBD-like colitis, complicated by severe COVID-19 and encephalopathy

required_evidence: History or documentation of defective T-cell proliferation (functional T-cell impairment)

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male warehouse forklift operator on night shift. You are evasive and speak minimally—short answers, often downplay symptoms. Start by saying: “I keep getting this tight feeling in my chest and I’m short of breath, especially when I’m walking or stressed.” Only volunteer: chest tightness/pressure that comes and goes, getting winded with activity that used to be easy, and unusual fatigue for a few weeks.

If asked directly about details, admit: pressure can spread to left shoulder/jaw, sometimes with cold sweat/nausea; lasts 5–15 minutes and improves with rest; occasional racing/skipping heartbeat; a few lightheaded near-faint episodes; sometimes wake up short of breath needing to sit up; ankles puffy by day’s end; more heartburn after spicy/late meals.

Withhold unless specifically probed: increased ibuprofen use, poor sleep/stress, heavy coffee/energy drinks, loud snoring/possible apnea, weight gain, and chest tightness after using rescue inhaler more than once.

Resist disclosure (deny/deflect at first): recent cocaine use, online “male enhancement” pills, erectile problems.

Express misconceptions: “It’s probably anxiety/indigestion/asthma or a pulled muscle.” Emphasize cost/insurance fears and avoiding time off. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay guarded.

lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO daily
atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly
ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
confounders: recent NSAID use (ibuprofen) contributing to acute kidney injury risk
diuretic (hydrochlorothiazide) and ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) use complicating renal perfusion during acute illness
poorly controlled diabetes increasing risk of infection and masking typical symptoms
possible dehydration from reduced oral intake contributing to elevated creatinine and lactate

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insufficient information to determine a single correct diagnosis (undifferentiated presentation)
required_evidence: At least one symptom or chief complaint (e.g., fever, cough, dyspnea, dysuria, abdominal pain, vomiting/diarrhea, polyuria/polydipsia, confusion)
Vital signs (temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation)
Focused history (onset, severity, exposures, sick contacts, travel, comorbidities, current medications, pregnancy status when applicable)
Focused physical exam findings relevant to suspected source (lung, abdomen, CVA tenderness, skin/soft tissue, neurologic status)
Basic metabolic panel interpretation including BUN and CO2 (bicarbonate) with clinical context
If infection suspected: source-directed testing (e.g., urinalysis, chest imaging) and lactate when available

expected_lab_results.WBC: Variable; may be elevated in infection/inflammation, normal in early infection, or low in severe sepsis/immunosuppression—cannot be predicted without symptoms/vitals
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Variable; may be normal, low in chronic disease/bleeding, or elevated with hemoconcentration from dehydration—cannot be predicted without context
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Variable; may be normal, low in sepsis/DIC, or high in inflammation—cannot be predicted without context
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Variable; may be low with GI losses/ADH-mediated hyponatremia or high with free water loss—cannot be predicted without context
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Variable; may be low with vomiting/diuretics or high with AKI/acidosis—cannot be predicted without context
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Variable; may be high in non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (diarrhea) or low with vomiting—cannot be predicted without context
expected_lab_results.CO2 (bicarbonate): Variable; may be low in metabolic acidosis (DKA, diarrhea, lactic acidosis) or high in metabolic alkalosis (vomiting)—cannot be predicted without context
expected_lab_results.BUN: Variable; may be elevated in dehydration/pre-renal azotemia or normal—cannot be predicted without context

medications: No empiric medication can be designated as correct without a defined diagnosis and clinical severity; avoid penicillins due to urticarial allergy unless formal allergy evaluation/desensitization is indicated
non_pharmacological: Obtain immediate vital signs and assess airway, breathing, circulation; escalate to emergency care if unstable
Clarify chief complaint and timeline; perform focused exam to identify likely source/system
Interpret available labs in clinical context; if dehydration/AKI suspected, assess volume status and consider oral/IV rehydration depending on severity
If infection suspected, obtain source-directed diagnostics (e.g., urinalysis/culture, chest imaging) and initiate guideline-directed therapy once source and severity are established
If metabolic acidosis suspected (low CO2), assess for DKA/toxic ingestion/sepsis; obtain glucose, anion gap, ketones, lactate as available
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate reassessment after history/vitals/exam and initial lab review (same encounter); disposition (ED vs outpatient) based on stability and suspected diagnosis

must_include: Current information is insufficient to name a single definitive diagnosis
Explain the leading possibilities based on symptoms/vitals/exam once obtained
Explain what the available labs can and cannot tell us without clinical context
Treatment depends on identifying the cause and assessing severity
If unstable or red-flag symptoms are present, need urgent/emergency evaluation
Avoid penicillin-class antibiotics due to history of hives unless specifically evaluated
Elicit patient goals and concerns and confirm understanding
Provide clear return precautions (e.g., worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, persistent vomiting, fainting, reduced urine output, high fever)
Discuss potential medication risks and allergy implications
Hydration guidance if GI illness/dehydration suspected (oral rehydration, monitoring urine output)
Avoid self-starting leftover antibiotics and the importance of targeted therapy
When to seek urgent care based on symptom progression

Order targeted initial labs based on undifferentiated presentation (e.g., CBC, CMP, urinalysis, pregnancy test if applicable, respiratory viral testing if respiratory symptoms, lactate/troponin/D-dimer only if clinically indicated) to assess severity and narrow differential.
Interpret results in clinical context; identify abnormalities suggesting dehydration, infection, metabolic derangement, organ dysfunction, or need for escalation.

Provide a working differential (not a single definitive diagnosis if insufficient data), explain leading possibilities, and outline next diagnostic/management steps and thresholds for urgent evaluation.
Before any prescribing, confirm and document allergy details and severity; specifically avoid penicillin-class antibiotics in history of hives unless formally evaluated.
Before prescribing, screen for clinically significant drug-drug interactions with current meds/supplements (including OTCs).
Before prescribing, assess contraindications based on comorbidities, pregnancy/lactation, renal/hepatic function, QT risk, age, and relevant lab abnormalities.
If indicated, prescribe the safest evidence-based therapy aligned to the working diagnosis and severity; avoid unnecessary antibiotics; ensure allergy-safe alternatives.
Explain diagnosis uncertainty, treatment plan, medication risks (including allergy implications), hydration guidance when relevant, and explicit return precautions.
Arrange appropriate follow-up based on severity and diagnostic uncertainty; include timeframe and what to monitor.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and ensuring safety netting is provided.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly, a bit worried about your breathing. Start by saying: “I’ve had a bad cough and I’m getting short of breath.” Voluntarily share: a dry cough worsening over 10 days, getting winded climbing one flight of stairs, low-grade fevers/chills off and on, and feeling very tired.

If asked, add: chest tightness with deep breaths, wheezing/whistling especially at night, night sweats, poor appetite with a few pounds of unintentional weight loss, mostly dry cough (no thick mucus), and that shortness of breath has worsened over the last 2–3 days.

Only reveal if specifically asked about triggers/meds/exposures: you started a new antibiotic last month for a skin infection, cleaned a dusty/moldy basement recently, began vaping recently, had a brief rash, and sometimes wake at night needing to sit up to catch your breath.

Resist discussing THC cartridges; deny/minimize unless directly and nonjudgmentally asked, then reluctantly admit. Say you thought it was asthma; you tried a friend’s inhaler without much help. Ask for antibiotics for “walking pneumonia.” Express concern about missing work, costs, and request cheaper options. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more details.

medications: Diethylcarbamazine 100 mg PO three times daily
confounders: Recent travel to a filariasis-endemic region with empiric diethylcarbamazine use, raising concern for parasitic infection-related pulmonary symptoms
Recent initiation of diethylcarbamazine (potential drug-induced eosinophilic lung disease vs infection)
History of atopy (allergic rhinitis) which may bias toward asthma/atopic disease rather than primary eosinophilic pneumonia
Low procalcitonin despite fever and leukocytosis, complicating differentiation from bacterial community-acquired pneumonia

correct_diagnosis.primary: Eosinophilic pneumonia
required_evidence: Peripheral eosinophilia (elevated absolute eosinophil count and/or eosinophil percent) in a compatible respiratory illness
Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP and/or ESR) supportive of active pulmonary inflammation
Elevated total IgE supporting allergic/eosinophilic phenotype (not required but supportive)
Exclusion of common alternative causes of pulmonary infiltrates/respiratory symptoms (e.g., bacterial pneumonia) and assessment for triggers (medications, parasitic exposure)

expected_lab_results.Absolute eosinophil count: Elevated (eosinophilia; commonly >500/µL, can be markedly increased)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal (may be mildly low if chronic inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible)

medications: Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg PO once daily (typical adult 40-60 mg daily) with taper over 2-6 weeks depending on clinical response
Diethylcarbamazine 6 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for 14-21 days (only if parasitic/filariasis-associated eosinophilic lung disease is suspected or confirmed)
non_pharmacological: Assess for and remove potential triggers (new medications, environmental/occupational exposures); avoid re-exposure if drug-induced suspected
Supportive care: oxygen as needed to maintain target SpO2 per local protocol
Evaluate for parasitic exposure risk and obtain appropriate stool/serologic testing if clinically indicated before initiating antiparasitic therapy
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 48-72 hours for symptom and eosinophil response; repeat CBC with differential and inflammatory markers within 1-2 weeks; earlier review if worsening dyspnea, hypoxemia, or fever

must_include: Condition involves eosinophils causing lung inflammation (not the same as typical bacterial pneumonia)
Discuss likely triggers/causes (including medications and parasites) and why further evaluation may be needed
Explain expected improvement timeline with treatment and what labs are being monitored
Role of corticosteroids as first-line therapy and need for taper (do not stop abruptly without guidance)
When diethylcarbamazine is appropriate (only if parasitic cause suspected/confirmed) and typical duration
Key side effects to watch for: steroids (hyperglycemia, mood changes, insomnia, infection risk); diethylcarbamazine (fever/rash/headache; potential inflammatory reactions in heavy parasite burden)
Review penicillin allergy and reassure that proposed medications are not penicillins; advise to report any new rash, hives, swelling, or breathing difficulty
Safety-net symptoms requiring urgent care: worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, blue lips, severe allergic reaction, persistent high fever
Avoid identified triggers/exposures; discuss travel/exposure history relevant to parasites if applicable
Adherence importance and follow-up labs/visits to confirm resolution and guide steroid taper
Infection prevention while on steroids (hand hygiene, seek care for signs of infection)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for eosinophilic pneumonia and rule out emergencies: dyspnea severity, cough, fever, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxemia symptoms; timing (acute vs subacute), asthma/atopy, smoking/vaping, new meds (esp. antibiotics, NSAIDs, anticonvulsants), occupational/environmental exposures, travel, freshwater/soil exposure, animal contacts, parasitic risk, immunosuppression, prior similar episodes.

Order initial evaluation to support diagnosis and assess severity/etiology: CBC with differential (eosinophils), CMP/LFTs, IgE, CRP/ESR; consider ABG or pulse oximetry if available; consider stool ova/parasites and Strongyloides serology if exposure risk; consider chest imaging if within benchmark scope.
Interpret results to confirm eosinophilia and inflammatory pattern, assess organ function before steroids/other therapies, and decide whether parasitic workup is needed prior to immunosuppression.

Make the working diagnosis of eosinophilic pneumonia, distinguishing it from typical bacterial pneumonia and acknowledging differential (drug-induced, parasitic, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, vasculitis).
Perform required critical safety check before prescribing; specifically confirm penicillin allergy details and ensure planned medications are not penicillins; document reaction type and severity.

Perform required critical safety check before prescribing: screen for contraindications to systemic corticosteroids (uncontrolled infection, severe uncontrolled diabetes, active GI bleed, psychosis) and to diethylcarbamazine if being considered (only with suspected/confirmed filarial infection; assess pregnancy, comorbidities, and risk of severe inflammatory reactions with heavy parasite burden).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: review current meds for interactions (e.g., steroids with anticoagulants, NSAIDs, diabetes meds; diethylcarbamazine with other agents as applicable).

Initiate first-line therapy with systemic corticosteroids and plan taper; only prescribe diethylcarbamazine if parasitic cause is suspected/confirmed and appropriate by exposure/workup; include monitoring plan and precautions.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment (steroids and when DEC is appropriate), side effects, allergy reassurance, safety-net symptoms, trigger avoidance, adherence, infection prevention while on steroids, and what labs/visits will be monitored.

Arrange timely reassessment to confirm clinical improvement, review lab trends (eosinophils, glucose if relevant), adjust steroid taper, and ensure resolution; provide earlier follow-up if symptoms worsen.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing return precautions, and ensuring follow-up plan is in place.

minimal_paths.description: Typical L1 case: suspected eosinophilic pneumonia without strong parasitic exposure; confirm eosinophilia, start corticosteroids with taper and monitoring, provide education and follow-up.

hypoxemia/red flag symptoms (cyanosis, syncope, chest pain)
travel/exposure history for parasites (screening even if negative)

Agent orders additional etiologic tests based on exposure (e.g., Strongyloides serology, stool O&P) and delays DEC pending results; steroids still first-line with close follow-up.

Agent emphasizes severity assessment and advises urgent evaluation (e.g., ED) if hypoxemic/red flags are present, while still completing education and follow-up planning within the benchmark constraints.

Thinks needing opioids for pain means he is becoming addicted, so he avoids asking for adequate pain control and underreports symptoms.
Believes hydroxyurea is "basically chemo" that will make him infertile and cause cancer, so he refuses it without discussing risks/benefits.

Worries that disclosing frequent pain crises will affect employment and insurance eligibility, so he minimizes symptoms.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 27-year-old male with sickle cell disease. You speak vaguely and are a bit evasive, especially about meds, substances, and where you got pain pills. Start with: “Really bad pain in my chest and upper back since last night, I can’t catch a full breath.” Volunteer that deep breaths make it worse and that it feels like your usual pain crisis, but the breathing is worse.

Only if asked, mention low-grade fever/chills, dry cough, wheeze/tight chest, low home oxygen/feeling winded to the bathroom, prior “acute chest,” rib tenderness with movement/pressing, and missed hydroxyurea doses due to nausea.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: poor hydration, long car ride with few stops, morning headaches/unrefreshed sleep, intermittent yellow eyes, dark urine, more frequent crises, prior “high lung blood pressure” on ultrasound.

If probed, admit you took extra pain pills from a friend because you fear being labeled drug-seeking; you smoke weed most nights; you miss appointments due to work/transport.

Express worries about bills and losing shifts. Mention misconceptions: chest pressure with radiation to left shoulder/jaw, fear of pneumonia or “organizing pneumonia.” If clinician is empathetic, soften and share more details.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sickle cell anemia (HbSS) with chronic hemolytic anemia
acceptable_alternatives: Sickle cell disease (HbSS) with steady-state hemolysis
Sickle cell anemia with compensated hemolysis
required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with chronic hemolytic anemia (low hemoglobin/hematocrit with elevated reticulocyte count)
Elevated RDW consistent with hemolysis/reticulocytosis
MCV typically normal unless concurrent folate deficiency or hydroxyurea use (not provided here)
No lab pattern suggesting acute bacterial pneumonia (would more typically show marked leukocytosis; diagnosis requires symptoms/imaging not provided)
Differentials listed (PAH, CAD, pneumonia, COP) cannot be confirmed from the available labs alone and require cardiopulmonary symptoms, imaging, ECG/echo, troponin, etc.

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Decreased (often ~6–9 g/dL in HbSS steady state)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Increased (reflecting marrow response to hemolysis; higher than normal baseline)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly increased at baseline in sickle cell disease (may be higher during stress/infection)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to increased (can be elevated due to functional asplenia/inflammation)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Typically normal (normocytic) unless folate deficiency or medication effect

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
non_pharmacological: Ensure adequate hydration and avoid triggers (extreme cold, high altitude, dehydration)
Update immunizations appropriate for functional asplenia (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib, annual influenza) per local schedule
Screen for and counsel on complications of sickle cell disease (stroke risk, pulmonary hypertension, nephropathy, retinopathy)

If symptomatic anemia or acute complication suspected: urgent evaluation for vaso-occlusive crisis, acute chest syndrome, aplastic crisis, or infection (requires symptoms/vitals/imaging beyond provided labs)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2–4 weeks (earlier if symptomatic or labs significantly worse than baseline); repeat CBC/reticulocyte count at follow-up or sooner if clinical change

must_include: Sickle cell anemia causes chronic hemolysis leading to low hemoglobin and a compensatory high reticulocyte count
Baseline labs in sickle cell disease can differ from people without SCD (e.g., lower hemoglobin, higher RDW/reticulocytes)
Some listed alternatives (pulmonary hypertension, coronary disease, pneumonia/COP) require symptom review and additional tests; labs alone are not diagnostic
Folic acid supports red blood cell production in chronic hemolysis
What symptoms should prompt urgent care (chest pain, shortness of breath, fever, neurologic deficits, severe pain crisis, marked fatigue/pallor)
Discuss that nicotinamide is not a standard therapy for sickle cell disease and should not replace disease-modifying management
Confirm penicillin allergy history (rash in childhood) and document reaction type/severity
Discuss infection risk with functional asplenia and importance of prompt evaluation for fever
Shared decision-making about hematology-directed disease-modifying options (e.g., hydroxyurea, transfusion strategies) if clinically indicated (not initiated solely from provided data)
Hydration, avoiding extreme temperatures/altitude, and early treatment of infections can reduce complications
Smoking avoidance and general cardiovascular risk reduction
Adherence to follow-up and routine screening for organ complications

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms and context to assess for vaso-occlusive crisis, acute chest syndrome, infection/sepsis risk, anemia severity, and alternative cardiopulmonary diagnoses; gather medication/supplement use and pregnancy status as applicable.
Required critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy details (reaction type/severity/timing) and document.

Order targeted evaluation if symptomatic or if diagnosis/complication status is unclear (e.g., CBC with differential, reticulocyte count, CMP, bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin, type & screen if transfusion considered; add infection or cardiopulmonary workup based on symptoms).
Review labs to support explanation of chronic hemolysis/baseline SCD patterns and to rule in/out acute complications or alternative diagnoses.

Synthesize findings: recognize baseline SCD hemolysis pattern vs acute complication; explicitly note that labs alone do not diagnose pulmonary hypertension/coronary disease/pneumonia/COP without symptom review and additional tests.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; evaluate interactions with current meds/supplements (including OTC and herbal products).

If appropriate, prescribe supportive therapy consistent with SCD care (e.g., folic acid) and avoid presenting non-standard agents (e.g., nicotinamide) as disease-modifying; ensure safety checks completed first.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and baseline labs, explain treatment rationale and urgent symptoms, address concerns (asplenia/infection risk, allergy), and provide lifestyle guidance.

Arrange hematology/primary care follow-up and screening for organ complications; ensure shared decision-making about disease-modifying options (e.g., hydroxyurea/transfusion strategies) without initiating solely from limited data.
Close encounter after ensuring safety-netting, follow-up plan, and patient understanding.

general_concerns: Believes his fevers are just 'caught from other kids' and not part of a medical condition, so he hides symptoms to avoid missing school or activities.
Thinks antibiotics should be taken whenever he has a fever, and stops them early if he feels better because 'too much medicine is bad for the liver.'
Believes vaccines can 'overload' the immune system and cause fevers, so he says he is 'allergic' to shots to avoid them.
Thinks iron supplements will always fix anemia because 'anemia means low iron,' and doesn’t understand why his anemia can persist despite iron.
Believes blood tests and transfusions are 'taking his blood away' and making him weaker, so he avoids appointments and downplays fatigue.
Thinks recurring infections are due to being 'dirty' or not trying hard enough to stay healthy, leading to shame and avoidance of details.
Believes developmental delays mean he is 'not smart' and will be punished at school, so he avoids answering questions and lets his caregiver speak.
economic_concerns: Worries that frequent clinic visits will make his caregiver miss work and lose income, so he minimizes symptoms and denies recent fevers or infections.
Believes specialist care and immune-related medicines are 'too expensive to be worth it' and assumes there are no assistance programs.
Thinks lab monitoring is unnecessary because it 'costs money and never changes anything,' so he resists blood draws and follow-up appointments.
Assumes that if he feels okay today, the family can skip medications or appointments to save money, even if the plan requires consistent care.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX’s caregiver (parent). You are worried but evasive and vague. Start with: “He keeps getting fevers over and over and he’s always tired.” Volunteer only: fevers that return every few weeks with chills then resolve on their own, low energy, pale appearance, slower speech/learning. Minimize symptoms at first due to cost/time: say “He’s fine today,” “maybe it’s just colds,” and resist labs/follow-ups (“blood tests cost money and never change anything,” “specialists/immune meds are too expensive”). If asked directly, disclose: mouth sores during fevers, tender neck glands, more lingering ear/sinus infections, easy bruising/tiny red spots after fevers, gets winded running, poor appetite/poor weight gain, left-sided belly fullness. Keep hidden unless specifically probed: triggers after minor colds/vaccines, irritability/light/noise sensitivity during fevers, occasional yellow eyes, darker stools after fever weeks, leg bone aches at night. Resist/avoid admitting: recurrent groin/buttock boils needing antibiotics, urine leakage at school during fever days, missed daily meds/supplements. Mention misleading joint puffiness/limp after fevers; wonder about “autoimmune” or “blood cycle problem.” If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly, share more, but still worry about costs.

diagnosis.primary: congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome
differentials: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital sideroblastic anemia–B-cell immunodeficiency–periodic fever–developmental delay syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: Juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1 (LACC1-associated systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis/autoinflammatory disease)
required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with congenital sideroblastic anemia (typically microcytic or dimorphic anemia with elevated RDW and inappropriately low reticulocyte response)
Recurrent periodic fevers and clinical history suggestive of autoinflammatory phenotype

History of recurrent infections consistent with humoral (B-cell) immunodeficiency (e.g., recurrent sinopulmonary infections, poor vaccine responses) even if immunologic labs are not provided in this task
Neurodevelopmental delay documented in history/exam
Exclusion/low likelihood of cyclic hematopoiesis based on absence of cyclic leukocyte/reticulocyte oscillations on serial CBCs
Exclusion/low likelihood of primary inflammatory arthritis as the sole diagnosis (no persistent inflammatory polyarthritis pattern; anemia pattern not explained by LACC1 disease alone)

expected_lab_results.RBC count: Normal to low (may be relatively preserved compared with hemoglobin in microcytosis)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Low (microcytosis) or dimorphic indices; overall tends toward low in sideroblastic anemia
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative/ineffective erythropoiesis)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Often normal between febrile episodes; may be elevated during fever/infection (no fixed pattern required)

medications: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO once daily
Folic acid 1 mg PO once daily
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/PR every 6 hours as needed for fever (max 60 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day, whichever is lower)
non_pharmacological: Hematology follow-up for congenital sideroblastic anemia: assess transfusion need, monitor iron overload; consider iron chelation if transfusion-dependent or evidence of overload

Immunology referral for B-cell immunodeficiency: evaluate immunoglobulin levels and vaccine responses; implement infection-prevention plan (vaccination strategy, prompt evaluation of fevers); consider immunoglobulin replacement if indicated
Fever plan: clear return precautions for persistent fever, signs of sepsis, dehydration, or respiratory distress
Developmental services: early intervention/therapy referrals (PT/OT/speech) and neurodevelopmental assessment
Avoid empiric iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is confirmed (risk of iron overload in sideroblastic anemia)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1–2 weeks with hematology/immunology (earlier within 24–72 hours if ongoing fever, worsening symptoms, or severe anemia)

must_include: Syndrome involves bone marrow making abnormal red cells (sideroblastic anemia) causing chronic anemia
Immune component (B-cell/humoral) increases risk of certain infections
Periodic fevers can be inflammatory and/or triggered by infections; fevers need careful evaluation
Developmental delay requires ongoing supportive therapies and monitoring
Purpose of pyridoxine: may improve anemia in some congenital sideroblastic anemias; response is monitored with CBC/reticulocytes
Folate supports red cell production; not a substitute for treating underlying disorder
Transfusions may be needed depending on symptoms/hemoglobin; discuss iron overload monitoring and chelation if applicable
Infection-prevention strategy and when immunoglobulin replacement might be used (after immunology evaluation)
Review antibiotic allergies: amoxicillin-clavulanate causes urticarial rash; vancomycin causes infusion-related flushing/pruritus (rate-related reaction)
Clarify that vancomycin reaction is typically managed with slower infusion/premedication if ever needed, but should be documented and discussed before use
Provide specific return precautions for fever (e.g., persistent >24–48h, lethargy, poor intake, breathing difficulty, signs of dehydration, or any concern for serious infection)
Avoid over-the-counter iron unless instructed by hematology
Keep vaccination records updated and follow immunology guidance (may differ if significant humoral immunodeficiency)
Track fever episodes (timing, duration, associated symptoms) to help distinguish periodic inflammation from infection
Encourage adherence to developmental therapies and school/IEP supports as appropriate

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current complaint, fever pattern, anemia symptoms, infection history, developmental status, meds/supplements, transfusion/chelation history, immunology care, and explicitly capture allergies and prior reaction details.

Order baseline evaluation tailored to anemia + fever/immunodeficiency: CBC with differential, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear, iron studies (ferritin, iron, TIBC/transferrin saturation), CMP/LFTs, CRP/ESR; if febrile/ill: blood culture, urinalysis/urine culture, respiratory viral PCR as indicated.
Interpret results to confirm anemia severity/type, assess marrow response, inflammation/infection markers, and iron overload risk.

Synthesize findings with known syndrome to generate a problem list: congenital sideroblastic anemia status, fever evaluation (infection vs inflammatory episodes), and B-cell immunodeficiency implications; identify urgency (e.g., severe anemia, sepsis risk).
Critical safety check before any prescribing; verify amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash and vancomycin infusion reaction details and documentation.
Non-critical but expected before prescribing to screen interactions (e.g., pyridoxine, folate, chelators, antibiotics, antipyretics) with current meds/supplements.

Initiate/continue evidence-aligned therapies: pyridoxine trial/continuation with monitoring plan; folate supplementation if indicated; transfusion plan if symptomatic or low Hb; infection management if suspected; avoid OTC iron unless directed; coordinate immunology for possible Ig replacement.

Deliver all required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale/monitoring, allergy clarification and fever return precautions, and lifestyle guidance (vaccines, iron avoidance, fever diary, developmental supports).
Arrange hematology and immunology follow-up, lab monitoring intervals (CBC/retic, ferritin), and clear plan for urgent reassessment with fever or worsening symptoms.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

Agent explains treatment elements: pyridoxine purpose and that response is monitored with CBC/reticulocytes; folate supports erythropoiesis but does not treat the underlying disorder; transfusions may be needed based on symptoms/hemoglobin with iron overload monitoring and chelation if applicable; infection-prevention strategy and when immunoglobulin replacement might be used after immunology evaluation.
Agent performs allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing; failure of either is a critical error.

Agent reviews and documents antibiotic allergy details: amoxicillin-clavulanate caused urticarial rash; vancomycin reaction is consistent with infusion-related flushing/pruritus (rate-related) and notes slower infusion/premedication approach if ever needed while still documenting and discussing prior to use.

Agent provides specific fever return precautions: persistent fever >24–48h, lethargy, poor intake, breathing difficulty, signs of dehydration, or any concern for serious infection; includes instruction to seek urgent care if immunodeficient child appears ill.

Agent advises avoiding over-the-counter iron unless instructed by hematology and discusses vaccination record maintenance with immunology guidance (acknowledging schedules may differ with significant humoral immunodeficiency).
Agent encourages tracking fever episodes (timing, duration, associated symptoms) and adherence to developmental therapies and school/IEP supports.
Agent avoids unsafe prescribing (e.g., empiric antibiotics without considering allergy history; iron supplementation without indication; dismissing fever in immunodeficiency).
Agent uses shared decision-making and checks understanding (teach-back or equivalent) before ending.

expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Usually normal (normocytic) in anemia of chronic inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis can occur with inflammation)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected/confirmed HIV immunodeficiency: symptoms of opportunistic infections, prior HIV testing, ART history, adherence barriers, sexual/substance history, pregnancy potential, comorbidities, current meds/supplements, and allergies (esp. sulfonamides).

Order confirmatory and baseline HIV labs: 4th-gen HIV Ag/Ab, HIV-1 RNA, CD4 count, HIV genotype (if ART-naive), CMP/creatinine, CBC, hepatitis A/B/C, STI screening, TB screening, toxoplasma IgG, cryptococcal Ag if indicated by symptoms/very low CD4, pregnancy test if applicable.
Obtain results needed to confirm diagnosis (positive 4th-gen + detectable HIV RNA) and stage immunodeficiency (CD4, viral load) to guide OI prophylaxis and ART selection.
Diagnose HIV infection and immunodeficiency severity; identify AIDS by CD4 criterion if CD4 <200 and note need for opportunistic infection prophylaxis.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm sulfonamide allergy (rash) and any other drug allergies to avoid TMP-SMX and other contraindicated agents.
Critical safety check before prescribing: review current meds/supplements for interactions with ART (e.g., polyvalent cations with INSTIs, rifamycins, anticonvulsants, St. John’s wort).
Critical safety check before prescribing: ensure no interactions with chosen PJP prophylaxis alternative (e.g., atovaquone) and any other planned meds.
Initiate ART as soon as possible (per guideline first-line regimen appropriate to labs/clinical context) and start PJP prophylaxis when CD4 <200 using atovaquone due to sulfonamide rash allergy.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis confirmation criteria, CD4 meaning and AIDS criterion, benefits of viral suppression; explain treatment goals, adherence, side effects, missed doses; confidentiality/partner notification; safer sex/harm reduction, U=U, and low-CD4 infection precautions.

Arrange close follow-up for ART start (e.g., 2–4 weeks), lab monitoring (viral load response, CD4 trend, safety labs), vaccination planning, and reassessment of prophylaxis discontinuation criteria once immune recovery occurs.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks completed, prescriptions issued appropriately, and follow-up plan and return precautions provided.

nl_assertions: Agent confirms HIV diagnosis requires both a positive 4th-generation HIV Ag/Ab test and detectable HIV RNA (or equivalent confirmatory algorithm) before labeling as confirmed HIV.
Agent explains CD4 count as immune strength and states CD4 <200 cells/µL indicates high risk for opportunistic infections and meets AIDS criterion by CD4.
Agent states that with effective ART, viral load can become undetectable and outcomes improve.
Agent recommends starting antiretroviral therapy as soon as possible and emphasizes daily adherence to suppress viral load.
Agent identifies that PJP prophylaxis is indicated when CD4 <200 and states TMP-SMX is first-line but should be avoided here due to sulfonamide rash allergy; selects atovaquone as an alternative.
Agent explicitly performs allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check before prescribing any medication; failure to do so is a critical error.
Agent discusses common ART side effects, what to do if they occur, and clear guidance on what to do if doses are missed.
Agent addresses confidentiality, partner notification options, and connects patient to support resources.
Agent provides safer sex and harm-reduction counseling and explains U=U requires sustained viral suppression while emphasizing adherence and follow-up.
Agent advises precautions when CD4 is low (food safety, infection exposure reduction) and provides return precautions for fever or respiratory symptoms.
Agent avoids unsafe or inaccurate claims (e.g., claiming U=U immediately after first dose, recommending TMP-SMX despite sulfa allergy, or omitting urgent evaluation for concerning symptoms).

minimal_paths.description: Confirm HIV infection and immunodeficiency stage, start ART promptly, and initiate PJP prophylaxis with atovaquone due to sulfonamide allergy; provide core counseling and follow-up.
must_collect: history of HIV testing/diagnosis and any prior ART
current symptoms (fever, cough, dyspnea, weight loss, thrush, diarrhea, headache/meningismus)
sulfonamide allergy details (rash) and other allergies
sexual history and transmission risk assessment
steps: ASK: targeted history for HIV confirmation context, OI symptoms, meds, allergies, adherence barriers, transmission risks
ORDER_LAB: 4th-gen HIV Ag/Ab, HIV RNA, CD4, baseline safety labs and co-infection screening
GET_RESULTS: confirm positive 4th-gen + detectable HIV RNA; note CD4 <200 if present
DIAGNOSE: HIV infection with immunodeficiency; AIDS by CD4 criterion if CD4 <200
CHECK_INTERACTION: review current meds/supplements for ART interactions
PRESCRIBE: start guideline-concordant ART; start atovaquone for PJP prophylaxis if CD4 <200

EDUCATE: explain diagnosis criteria, CD4 meaning, ART goals, prophylaxis rationale, side effects, adherence, confidentiality/partner notification, safer sex/U=U, low-CD4 precautions and return precautions
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: early follow-up for tolerance/adherence and repeat HIV RNA/safety labs

acceptable_variants.description: Agent starts ART the same day after ordering baseline labs (including genotype) without waiting for all results, provided confirmatory HIV RNA is available/ordered and safety checks (allergy/interactions/contraindications) are completed before prescribing; follow-up arranged to adjust regimen if needed.
steps: GET_RESULTS (or document pending but confirmatory testing ordered and no conflicting data)
PRESCRIBE (ART + atovaquone for PJP prophylaxis if CD4 <200)

acceptable_variants.description: If CD4 result is pending but there is strong suspicion of advanced immunodeficiency (e.g., prior documented CD4 <200 or clear OI risk), agent discusses and may initiate PJP prophylaxis while expediting CD4 testing, still avoiding TMP-SMX due to sulfonamide rash allergy and completing safety checks first.
steps: PRESCRIBE (ART; consider empiric atovaquone if high-risk while CD4 pending)
